_note: Runtime-robustness checkpoints. Cross-domain — any app with a backend reuses these.

checkpoints.title: Failures are handled and never leak internals to the client
checkpoints.why: Generated code tends to swallow errors (empty catch), let promise rejections go unhandled, or return raw exceptions - so failures pass silently OR spill stack traces, SQL, and secrets to the user. Both are bad: one hides real faults, the other hands an attacker a map.

levels.3: Errors swallowed by empty catches, left as unhandled rejections, or returned to the client as raw exceptions/stack traces.
levels.6: Errors are caught and logged server-side; clients get a generic message with an appropriate status code; no stack trace, SQL, or secret in the response.
levels.9: Structured logging with correlation ids, typed error boundaries, alerting on error-rate spikes, and graceful degradation when a dependency fails.

verify.reasoning: Are errors swallowed by empty catch blocks or left as unhandled rejections? When a request fails, does the client receive a generic message with a proper status code, or a raw stack trace / SQL / internal detail? Are failures logged server-side?

assertions.check: Errors are not silently swallowed; failures are logged server-side.
Client-facing errors are generic (no stack trace, SQL, or secret) with an appropriate status code.
